
# Task Six

## Intended Learning Outcomes

* Report an analysis and write-up for the Warbler data

> This data is best suited for analysis by a Binomial GLM

* **Work in pairs** to make this project reproducible, clear and interpretable


## Outcomes

* Produce a short qmd document with an Analysis Section & Results Section

* Include one figure and one table

* Produce a project with clear directories, well-named scripts

* Make sure scripts run without error, have clear comments and are well laid out

## Scripts

- Use a style guide to help you https://style.tidyverse.org/

- Use AI support if needed (declare all uses and record prompts)

- Make sure to proof and check all code produced by human or AI

## Tips

- The dependent variable here is 0 or 1 - which means it can be as a variable directly in the model (no need for cbind)

- You should perform EDA to decide if any interactions should be included

- You may find there is some multicollinearity - what do you do?

- Set character strings to appropriate factors

## Analysis

- Briefly describe any data cleaning tasks required - that affect the analysis (imputation of missing data, removal of duplicates) *standardising variable names or changing cases is less important*.

- Clearly describe your statistical approach: What type of model did you use? Why was it appropriate for your data?

- Explain your variables: What are the dependent and independent variables? Were any interactions or covariates included, and why?

- Detail the software and packages used (include version number): What tools did you use for data cleaning, modeling, and visualisation?

- Mention model assumptions

## Results

- State the main findings concisely: What were the key results, and were they statistically significant?

- Provide relevant statistical values: Include test statistics, degrees of freedom, P-values, confidence intervals, and effect sizes where applicable.

- Use tables and figures effectively: Summarize results using well-labeled tables and reference them in the text.

- Compare your findings: How do your results relate to your hypothesis? Did any unexpected patterns emerge?

- Interpret the biological significance: Beyond statistical significance, what do these findings mean in the context of your study?
